California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Clayton P. Roche 
Deputy Attorney General 
Department of Justice 
August 13, 1987 
350 McAllister street, Room 6000 
San Francisco, CA 
94102 
Re: 
Your Opinion No. 87-602; Our File No. 
1-87-1.96w~ 
-, 
Dear Mr. Roche: 
You have written soliciting the Commission's views regarding 
a pending opinion request from the city of Los Angeles. 
The 
request seeks your office's input regarding the interrelation-
ship of Elections Code section 11800 and Los Angeles city 
Charter section 3l2(c) (1). 
The Commission has no views 
regarding that specific question. 
However, we do wish to 
apprise you of the fact that section 11800 has been repealed by 
Chapter 230, Statutes 1987. 
This repeal will be effective 
January 1, 1988. 
The Commission will soon begin considering possible 
amendments to its regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code section 18901 now 
that both state and local government officials are covered by 
the provisions of Government Code section 89001. 
I trust that you will find this information useful in your 
endeavors. 
Should you have any questions, please do not 
hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901. 
REL:km 
Sincerely, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
~neral Counsel

Our opinion has been requested as follows with respect to section 
11800 of the Elections Code as it may pertain to candidates for 
various city offices in Los Angeles. 
"Section 11800 prohibits mass mailings at public expense 
after an elected official has filed a declaration of candidacy, 
'or any other paper evidencing an intention to be a candidate for 
any local, state, or federal office.' 
"Los Angeles' political reform law, which was enacted in 
1985, provides that candidates for office before soliciting and 
receiving campaign contributions must file a 'declaration of 
intent to solicit and receive contributions in connection with 
candidacy for specific elective City office.' 
(Los Angeles City 
Charter, Section 312 {c} (1)). 
The question that I would like 
you to research is whether the filing of this 'declaration of 
intent to solicit and receive contributions' constitutes a 'paper 
evidencing an intention to be a candidate for any local •.. 
office' and, therefore, would prohibit the sending of newsletters 
or other mass mailing at p~blic eXpcnsc.~ 
In accordance with our policy to request views of interested 
parties and associations, you are invited to submit whatever 
views you may have on this request. 
Very truly yours, 
JOHN K. VAN DE KAMP 
Attorney General

§ 11800. 
Mass mailings at public expense; prohibition 
No newsletter or other mass mailing shaII be sent at public ex-
. pense by or in behalf of an elected city, county, or district official, in-
cluding an official of a chartered city or chartered county, to any 
person residing within the jurisdiction from which he or she was 
elected or to which he or she seeks election, after the elected official 
has filed a declaration of candidacy, nomination paper, affidavit of 
acceptance of sponsor's declaration, acceptance of nomination, or any 
other paper evidencing an intention to bE> a candidate for any local, 
state, cr fedel'al office. 
(Added by Stats.1976. c. 1260, § 1.)

"Mass mailing" means identical or nearly identical pieces of mall ,; 
in an amount which, in any month, exceeds 1 percent of the popula-
tion of the jurisdiction from which the official was elected, according 
to the latest federal census, or which, in the 30 days preceding a local 
election, exceeds 200 pieces, but does not include a form letter or oth-
er mail which is sent in response to a letter or inquiry or an official 
mailing required by law. 
(Added by Stats.1976, c. 1260, § 1.)

sections.question: Whether the filing of a 'declaration of intent to solicit and receive contributions' under Los Angeles City Charter Section 312(c)(1) constitutes a 'paper evidencing an intention to be a candidate for any local office' under Elections Code section 11800, and therefore would prohibit the sending of newsletters or other mass mailings at public expense.
sections.conclusion: The Commission has no views regarding that specific question. However, the Commission does wish to apprise the Deputy Attorney General that section 11800 has been repealed by Chapter 230, Statutes 1987, effective January 1, 1988. The Commission will soon begin considering possible amendments to its regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code section 18901 now that both state and local government officials are covered by Government Code section 89001.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a Los Angeles city candidate's filing of a declaration of intent to solicit campaign contributions qualify as the type of paper that triggers Elections Code section 11800's prohibition on mass mailings by elected officials?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declined to offer an opinion on the specific interrelationship question because Elections Code section 11800 has been repealed effective January 1, 1988. The FPPC indicated it will soon consider amending its regulations to address the expanded coverage of state and local officials under Government Code section 89001.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple components: (1) FPPC's advisory response letter dated August 13, 1987; (2) the Attorney General's initial opinion request dated June 22, 1987; (3) statutory text of Elections Code sections 11800-11802; and (4) internal FPPC memorandum declining to research the matter. The primary FPPC response is the advisory letter which declines to offer substantive views but provides notice of statutory repeal. Confidence reduced slightly due to somewhat unclear structural relationship between documents.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the filing of a 'declaration of intent to solicit and receive contributions' under Los Angeles City Charter Section 312(c)(1) constitutes a 'paper evidencing an intention to be a candidate for any local office' under Elections Code section 11800, and therefore would prohibit the sending of newsletters or other mass mailings at public expense.

CONCLUSION: The Commission has no views regarding that specific question. However, the Commission does wish to apprise the Deputy Attorney General that section 11800 has been repealed by Chapter 230, Statutes 1987, effective January 1, 1988. The Commission will soon begin considering possible amendments to its regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code section 18901 now that both state and local government officials are covered by Government Code section 89001.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responded to the Attorney General's request for views on whether a Los Angeles candidate's filing of a declaration of intent to solicit contributions triggers Elections Code section 11800's mass mailing prohibition. The FPPC provided no substantive opinion on the question but instead advised that section 11800 has been repealed effective January 1, 1988.
